museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: May is a waiting room for summer arcades and autumn console hardware.
museum.curatorNote: No confident May-only release anchor is forced.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: Five researched moments, with broad context separated from confident month-level claims.

items.title: The Mega Drive is still a promise
items.body: Sega's 16-bit console exists as anticipation before its Japanese launch.
items.sourceNote: Mega Drive launches October 29, 1988.

items.title: UK magazines bridge too many worlds
items.body: One issue may need to cover Spectrum tapes, Amiga disks and Japanese console rumours.
items.sourceNote: UK magazine context.

items.title: Home-computer ambition grows
items.body: Carrier Command and RPGs show computer games becoming broad systems, not just screens.
items.sourceNote: 1988 computer-game context.

items.title: Arcade horror and driving drama are still ahead
items.body: Splatterhouse and Chase H.Q. will make November feel darker and faster.
items.sourceNote: November arcade context.

items.title: No confident May-only release anchor
items.body: The month is not padded with weak claims.
items.sourceNote: No strong May-specific release anchor found.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: Eight notable games from the year, led by month-specific anchors where the evidence supports them.

items.title: Super Contra
items.body: Konami's sequel pushes run-and-gun action harder, though exact early arcade timing varies.
items.sourceNote: 1988 is secure; month varies across sources.

items.title: Gradius II
items.body: Konami revisits the power-up-bar shooter with more spectacle and confidence.
items.sourceNote: Giant Bomb cites March 24, 1988.

items.title: Wasteland
items.body: Interplay's post-apocalyptic RPG becomes a long shadow over later computer RPGs.
items.sourceNote: MobyGames gives 1988; exact first date remains source-variable.

items.title: Pool of Radiance
items.body: SSI's first Gold Box AD&D game brings Forgotten Realms tactics to home computers.
items.sourceNote: MobyGames gives 1988; month not asserted.

items.title: Altered Beast
items.body: Sega's mythic transformation brawler becomes closely tied to the Mega Drive's launch image.
items.sourceNote: June 1988 is widely cited for the arcade version.

items.title: Carrier Command
items.body: Realtime Games mixes strategy, vehicles and islands into a 16-bit technical showcase.
items.sourceNote: MobyGames gives 1988; month not asserted.

items.title: Last Ninja 2
items.body: System 3 sends the ninja into a bigger, more confident sequel.
items.sourceNote: 1988 is secure; exact month not asserted.

items.title: Super Mario Bros. 3
items.body: Nintendo's platform sequel makes the Famicom feel impossibly polished.
items.sourceNote: Japanese release date is widely cited as October 23, 1988.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: Four machines or technology contexts that explain the month's place in gaming history.

items.title: Sega Mega Drive
items.body: Launched in Japan on October 29, Sega's 16-bit console marks a new phase even before Britain feels it.
items.sourceNote: Sega cites October 29, 1988.

items.title: Famicom / NES
items.body: Super Mario Bros. 3 and late-year action games show Nintendo's 8-bit machine at a peak.
items.sourceNote: Used for SMB3, Ninja Gaiden and Mega Man 2.

items.title: Amiga and Atari ST
items.body: 16-bit home computers make screenshots, music and simulation ambition central to the UK magazine experience.
items.sourceNote: Used for Carrier Command and UK aspiration context.

items.title: UK 8-bit micros
items.body: Spectrum, C64 and CPC remain everyday machines even as 16-bit hardware and Japanese consoles dominate headlines.
items.sourceNote: Used for UK lived-experience framing.

sections.title: Magazine Covers

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: Before online gaming was ordinary, paper, shops and local conversations carried the culture.

items.title: Online life is still a side-room
items.body: Compunet, Micronet, BBSs and modem culture exist, but most players remain offline.

items.title: Magazine previews are the browser window
items.body: A reader can travel from Mega Drive to Amiga to Spectrum without leaving the newsagent.

items.title: Regional release gaps create mythology
items.body: Games can be famous before they are locally available.

items.title: Letters pages create slow communities
items.body: Machine loyalty, review arguments and high-score claims move through print over weeks.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: A short atmospheric reading of the month as a player might have met it.

items.title: The next generation was visible but distant
items.body: May 1988 could show you the Mega Drive and Super Mario Bros. 3 in print while your own evening still belonged to a cassette or floppy disk.

items.title: Screenshots carried desire
items.body: Amiga, ST, arcade and Japanese console images made the future feel glossy and expensive.

items.title: The UK bedroom remained stubbornly practical
items.body: For all the hardware drama elsewhere, British players still loaded games, swapped tapes and read reviews by format.

items.title: Arcades became mood pieces
items.body: Splatterhouse, Chase H.Q. and Altered Beast made cabinets feel darker, faster and stranger.

editorNotes: Initial AI-generated research draft for May 1988.
Month-specific dates are used only where the reviewed sources support them; uncertain items are marked in sourceNote/certainty fields.
Magazine cover images are reconstructed placeholders and should be replaced with verified/licensed scans if used editorially.
